chapters.title: Sibling rivalry happens even with the best parenting
chapters.text:
Stewart Stafford once summed up sibling rivalry with a sharp line. The oldest child is the golden one, the middle child is invisible, and the youngest is the loud one. That line captures the struggle of parents with more than one child. Can rivalry, competition, jealousy, and fighting be avoided?

Tension between kids in one family comes from many emotional factors. One child may feel less loved, may feel unsafe, or may be in constant competition with a sibling. These fights happen even when parents are doing their best.

Sibling rivalry does not define who your children are. It is okay to see your kids compete.

As a parent, your job is to understand why rivalry happens and to avoid blaming your own parenting for every fight. You also need real techniques for handling the tension and a strategy for raising confident kids.

Are you ready to learn what it takes to shift your parenting? Keep reading.

chapters.title: Don't repress negative feelings
chapters.text:
Kids often feel threatened by the mere presence of a sibling, and that can flare into outright rage. You see them scream at each other, and they come running to you to tattle. The urge to yell back or block out their feelings is strong, just to end the noise. But brushing past their anger teaches them to push it down, and that buried anger can show up as physical symptoms or emotional problems. If you want a real fix, accept their emotions and treat them with respect. Kids feel safer when someone is willing to sit with the hard feelings.

Teaching your children to label their feelings helps them build healthy habits around emotion.

Letting your kids voice their feelings about a sibling does not mean you agree with them. It does mean you can stop the hurtful behavior and show them a better way to express anger. Let them know they can show displeasure without causing damage. Faber and Mazlish give a clear example of how to handle this.

Instead of saying something like, that is a terrible thing to do to the baby, she only touched your toys, show your child a better way by saying, no fighting. Tell your brother or sister how angry you are with words, not fists.

The second statement tells your child what to do when angry, while the first only teaches them that anger itself is bad. The goal is to give kids healthy ways to cope with frustration. Patience matters, and you have to model the behavior you want to see.

Adele Faber and Elaine Mazlish have said that we let children express all their feelings, we do not let them hurt each other, and our job is to show them how to express anger without damage.

chapters.title: Constant comparison increases anxiety
chapters.text:
One of the easiest ways parents stir up rivalry is by holding their kids up against each other. You hear lines like, your sister is so responsible, you would never see her room a mess, or, your brother never kicks the ball into the long grass, and you never seem to finish what you start.

Many parents promise themselves, before they even marry, that they will never compare their kids. Then they end up doing exactly that.

How do you break the habit. Be deliberate. Talk to each child on their own without dragging the siblings into the picture. Instead of measuring one child against another, focus on what you see in this child alone. Describe what they did, what you like about it, what you do not like, or what still needs to be done. Stay with that child's behavior. What their siblings are up to is not part of the conversation. For example, instead of saying, this is awful, even your younger brother does not spill all over himself, you can simply say, I see a little milk dripping down your dress.

A home that rewards cooperation helps kids function well, speak up for themselves, and reach the goals that matter to them.

Sometimes one child shares a small win in front of the others. How do you cheer them on without hurting the rest. Reflect their feelings back in front of everyone, and save the bigger praise for a private moment. If a child brings you a drawing while the siblings watch, say something like, wow, you must be so proud of yourself, rather than, I am thrilled, this is the best painting ever, I cannot wait to show everyone.

For context, a 2021 survey of two thousand adults, run for the UK television show Succession, found that 51 percent of those polled were still locked in a competitive relationship with their siblings.

chapters.title: Equality vs. Unique attitude
chapters.text:
Children are not mature enough to want true fairness, even though they often ask for it, and many parents fall into the trap. At Faber and Mazlish's sibling rivalry workshop, parents often describe how hard it is to keep things even between their kids.

One mother told a story about a fairy tale she read to her two children, Billy and Roy. Both loved the story, but Roy started sobbing afterward, since the tale was all about Bill. She promised to track down a version with Roy in it, yet Roy would not stop crying. The challenge was clear. How can a parent read a fairy tale without leaving one child feeling left out.

When your child asks who you love more, tell them they are each special because they are different.

Kids act like they want equal treatment, but the truth is that they do not. So instead of giving your children the same amount of food, drink, or attention, give them what each one needs. Showing them they are loved for who they are, not for being equal, teaches them how to handle the uneven parts of life.

For children, love is not about the exact amount of time or affection. It is about feeling like they matter as themselves. For example, do not say, you can have as many pancakes as your brother. Instead, ask, do you want a half pancake or a whole one. Rather than trying to spread love evenly with a line like, I love you both just the same, show them a unique kind of love. You can say, dear, you are such a special person. There is no other you in the universe, and I love you.

Adele Faber and Elaine Mazlish have said that to be loved for who you are is to be loved as much as anyone needs to be loved.

Make sure each of your kids feels like your favorite by respecting and responding to their differences.

chapters.title: Even positive roles can be damaging
chapters.text:
One of the authors was out in the driveway with her dog one evening. The neighbor's kids came running up with their mother close behind, shouting doggy, doggy as they approached. The author was glad both children liked dogs. The mother then said the younger one was bold enough to play with the dog, while the older one liked dogs but stayed back. She went on, complaining that the kids had been this way since birth, calling the younger one bold and the older one shy. She did not see that she was putting her kids into boxes and quietly building resentment between them.

Saying negative things about your children in front of them lowers their self-esteem and shapes how they handle relationships later in life.

Locking kids into roles can hurt them on their own and in how they get along with others, and it is one of the main reasons siblings fight. A few things drive parents to assign roles, including the urge to project their own weak spots onto their kids, the bad habit of building one child up by putting the other down, and the comfort of believing they have each child fully figured out.

Parents are not the only ones who do this. Kids put themselves into boxes too. A child may take on the role of the good boy to win a parent's love. Kids also push each other into roles. A woman at the workshop said her small, skinny older son bragged about his strength and called his younger brother weak. The younger son, who was actually well-built, took the comments to heart and started acting that way. When anyone asked him to lift something heavy, he would say he could not, since it was too much. He had no real sense of his own strength, and that false belief would be hard to undo if the teasing kept up.

Do not lock your kids into roles. To raise strong individuals, encourage them to explore who they are, test their strengths, and reach goals they did not know they could.

chapters.title: What to do when kids fight
chapters.text:
Children fight for all kinds of reasons. They fight over toys, they fight because they are bored, and some just want to see how their parents will react. The best move when your kids quarrel is to stay out of it and let them work it out. The more you step in, the more they pull you in, and the less they learn to settle things on their own. Still, you should step in when they are in danger. Start by describing what you see, and then separate them.

Do not try to settle your kids' fights. Help them get past the communication block so they can find their own answers.

Here are a few ways to handle their quarrels. Acknowledge their anger toward each other. Give each child your full attention and listen well. Show you appreciate the moment without mocking their age. Tell them you know they are smart enough to find a fair solution. Then leave them alone to keep talking.

Sometimes, kids are upset about how a sibling treats them but cannot bring themselves to tell a parent. Most say their parents will not listen or think they are being dramatic. It is worth getting past that first reaction and helping them work things out.

In that case, start by meeting with the children who are having trouble and explain why you called them in. Let the child with the problem speak first without interruption, then let the other child give their side. As each child shares, write it down and read it back so they know you got it right. Then give each child a chance to respond, and let everyone suggest ways to fix the problem. Let the kids speak first, and write down all their ideas without judging them. Pick the solutions that everyone can live with, and set a follow-up meeting to see how things are going.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Sibling relationships shift in ways you cannot fully predict. Sisters and brothers may start with a hostile attitude and grow closer later in life. They may also drift apart as they move away from their parents.

Parents cannot force a fixed, close, loving bond between their children. The best they can do is gently clear the barriers that might stop their kids from building a deeper connection when the time is right.

Try this. Ask your kids to write down things they admire about their siblings. Set up a calm family meeting to talk about family concerns, where each person's view is welcome and respected. Plan personal one-on-one time with each child so they get your full attention.
